title: K-Flip

content.problemStatement:
You are given a binary string S=S1S2…SNS = S_1S_2 \ldots S_NS=S1​S2​…SN​ of length NNN. You are also given an integer KKK. You can do the following operation at most N−K+1N-K+1N−K+1 times:


First, choose a substring SiSi+1…Si+K−1S_iS_{i+1} \ldots S_{i+K-1}Si​Si+1​…Si+K−1​ of SSS of length exactly KKK. This substring shouldn't have been chosen in any of the previous operations. That is, the same starting index of the substring can't be chosen twice.
Then, flip all the characters of the chosen substring (i.e. change every 000 to 111 and vice versa).
Find the lexicographically smallest string possible after the operations if you do them in an optimal way.

**Note**:


A substring is consecutive elements of a string. For eg. in the string "01011", "101" is a substring, but "111" is not a substring.
A string AAA is lexicographically smaller than string BBB, if Ai<BiA_i < B_iAi​<Bi​, where iii is the first index where AAA and BBB differ.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers: NNN, KKK.
The second line of each test case contains the string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the lexicographically smallest string that you can achieve.
constraints: S consists only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains two integers N and K. The second line contains a binary string S of length N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the lexicographically smallest binary string that can be obtained after performing at most N‑K+1 operations as described.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Applying the greedy flip strategy yields the smallest possible strings for each test case.
Flipping each possible length‑2 substring turns all bits to 0.
A single flip changes the only '1' to '0'.

solution.approach: We process the string from left to right keeping track of how many flips are currently affecting the current position. Using a difference array (or lazy propagation) we can apply a flip of length K in O(1) time: when we decide to flip at position i we toggle a running parity variable and schedule a toggle at i+K to cancel the effect later. At each index we compute the actual bit after all previous flips. If the bit is '1' and we still have room to start a K‑length flip (i <= N-K), we flip to make it '0' (which is always optimal for lexicographic minimality). This greedy choice is optimal because any '1' that can be turned into '0' earlier will always lead to a smaller string, and flips do not interfere with earlier positions. The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case with O(N) auxiliary space for the difference array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space per test case for the difference array.
